Request Form, 3-Ha 15 8375 Yoo Jimin. Balance inquiry for inmate funds.

It was the afternoon schedule on the 14th day of Yoo Jimin’s prison life. After checking the report form written on a palm-sized scrap of paper, Minjeong let out a small groan. The prisoner in charge of delivering the forms for Block 3-Ha fidgeted with his fingers, eyeing Minjeong cautiously.

"I told her clearly that it wasn't possible anymore... but she insisted that she lost it... and that she absolutely had to check again..."

"Hmm... alright. You’ve worked hard."

The report form was the official written application used by inmates to communicate with correctional officers within the facility. It was the standard way to request everything from private interviews with the officer in charge or medical attention, to trivial but necessary tasks like checking the balance of inmate funds for purchasing daily necessities. Usually, the inmate messenger collected them at designated times—morning, noon, and evening—and delivered them to the officer, but according to regulations, simple inquiries like balance checks could not be issued more than twice a day. Yet, this was the third request today alone. Twice in the morning, and now again. No matter how absent-minded someone might be, it didn't make logical sense to lose a receipt three times a day. And it had been the same pattern for nearly a week. Minjeong bit her lower lip, trying to grasp Jimin’s intentions. Was she pulling some 'chaebol' power trip? Her stomach churned with irritation, wondering if Jimin saw her as an easy mark just because she had felt bad for her and tried to be accommodating.

For ordinary documents, it would have sufficed to have them delivered through the commissary, but inmate fund receipts were the exception. As they contained sensitive personal information directly linked to an inmate’s private property, they had to be delivered personally by the officer in charge to prevent any mishaps. In other words, every time Jimin submitted a request form, Minjeong had to take the receipt and walk all the way to the front of cell 3H-15. The funny thing was, on days when Minjeong was off duty following a night shift, there wasn’t a single request for a receipt. At Taeseong Prison’s 3H Ward, four teams took turns covering the cell block. One sergeant and two officers formed a set responsible for all day and night shifts in the ward, and Minjeong’s team included Sergeant Kim Jaesook and Officer Oh Habin. While the principle was for team members to manage the entire cell block collectively, they often divided the hallways into sectors for more efficient control. Jimin’s cell, 3H-15, was included in Minjeong’s designated sector. On days Minjeong’s team was off, another team took over, and even if Minjeong was on duty but away from her post, her partner Habin could have handled it—yet Jimin strictly wanted only Minjeong. Habin, who was organizing documents at the desk next to her, burst into a giggle and teased Minjeong.

"Officer Kim, don't you think you've been targeted by Yoo Jimin? It looks like she's bothering you on purpose."

"But she says the reason is that she keeps losing them, so I can't really say anything. Ugh, the more I think about it, the more annoyed I get. Why doesn't she submit a single form when other teams are on duty, but it's always this way when I'm here?"

Minjeong adjusted the collar of her duty uniform irritably. She felt a complicated mix of thoughts—was she really being harassed, as Habin suggested, or was there another motive? Everything was quiet on the days she wasn’t there, but the moment she arrived at work, stacks of request forms flew in as if they had been waiting for her. Minjeong suppressed her resentment, pressing it into the twice-folded deposit receipt, and opened the iron door leading to the 3H hallway. She vowed that this time, she would give her a stern talking-to.

When Minjeong reached cell 3H-15 and rapped on the window, the inmates who had been lying in the corner watching TV jumped up in shock. Damin and Sejin, without a word, grabbed cleaning rags and pretended to wipe the floor, while Miyeon scrambled clumsily across the short distance of the small room to stand by the window. During work hours, lying down or sleeping in the living area was strictly prohibited. Miyeon rattled off a nonsensical excuse about how she had been doing sit-ups with her older sisters. Minjeong, whose annoyance had already reached its limit because of Jimin, didn't even have the energy to scold Miyeon.

"Where is Yoo Jimin?"

"Jimin-unnie went to the restroom!"

Minjeong tilted her head slightly to check the restroom in the corner of the cell. Beyond the partition—transparent at the top and translucent at the bottom—Jimin’s silhouette was faintly visible as she crouched, seemingly washing her hands. The awkward restroom design, a compromise between inmate human rights and surveillance efficiency, laid Jimin’s busy movements bare.

"Ah... I see. When she comes out, give this to her. It’s a deposit receipt, so tell her not to unfold it."

She had meant to say something, but not wanting to wait, she pushed the folded receipt through the bars.

"Oh! Oh! I'm here! Officer, I'm right here!"

The restroom door swung open, and Jimin walked to the window, shaking the water off her wet hands. She pushed Miyeon, who was standing beside her, toward the back and thrust both hands through the bars. It was absurd. She was testing her patience. Minjeong placed the deposit receipt onto Jimin’s hands—which were small and cute, quite unlike her personality—and hardened her expression.

"Yoo Jimin-ssi. Since these deposit receipts are personal items, why don’t you keep them properly in your storage bin? Stop submitting request forms saying you lost them every time."

Unmistakable fatigue and sharp barbs laced her tone. However, Jimin didn't blink an eye; she clutched the receipt and replied.

"I definitely put it inside the plastic storage bin, but it’s gone."

"Does that even make sense?"

"It’s true. It just vanished into thin air, I swear."

Jimin opened her eyes wide as if to show her innocence, staring directly at Minjeong. She didn’t look like she was lying, but it was too absurd a claim to believe. Minjeong massaged her brow, feeling a stiff pain rising up the back of her neck. Irritation kept surging up.

"Then I’m going to conduct a brief cell inspection."

".....Yes?"

"That’s fine, right?"

"If I say it's not... you won't do it?"

Jimin asked in a small, shrinking voice, but Minjeong was already clutching the key card hanging from her waist.

"Hardly. I'm not asking for your permission."

"U-um?... Then why did you ask if it was fine?"

Meeting Jimin's face head-on as she looked at her with an expression like she was about to burst into tears, Minjeong placed her hand on the radio transmitter fixed to her shoulder.

"Chief, Unit 3-15 has an anomaly. Initiating emergency inspection."

A short reply came back through the static: "Understood. Keep yourself safe." Jimin shook her head violently and stepped back, while the inmates of 3-15 hurriedly rummaged through their bins, fearing something might be found among their belongings. As the commotion spread, the sounds of inmates in cells 14 and 16 moving busily, wondering if their rooms would be inspected too, filled the air chaotically. Minjeong held her key card to the reader on the iron door. As soon as the lock disengaged, she swung the heavy door open. "3-15, everyone, get out into the corridor immediately." Minjeong stood firmly in front of the door, clearing a path for the inmates.

"Come out and line up against the left wall. Hands on the wall, and keep your distance from the person next to you. Yu Jimin-ssi, go to the very end."

The inmates of 3-15 spilled out into the corridor and stood in a line, hands pressed against the wall. Minjeong shoved Jimin’s back as she hesitantly trailed out, forcing her to join the end of the line. Jimin’s thin back was felt distinctly through her palm. For a brief, fleeting moment, Minjeong was swept by a strange sensation.

"No turning around or talking to the person next to you until the inspection is over. Do you understand?"

Pulling a pair of crisp latex gloves from the equipment belt at her waist, Minjeong tightened them over each finger joint and stepped into the exposed 3-15 cell. Suppressed voices leaked out from among the inmates plastered against the wall. *Look at what you’ve caused because of your unnie, seriously. I told you to stop. Just because you're a chaebol, you have to show off your money with those deposits? Why do you keep getting receipts and getting us all raided.* The sound of them berating Jimin drilled into her ears. Under normal circumstances, she would have ordered silence, but at Jimin’s following reply, Minjeong almost burst out laughing.

"Hweng... I’m sooory."

It was because Jimin’s apology, mumbled in a completely deflated tone, was far more pathetic and cute than expected. To think a chaebol would be placed in a shared cell and end up getting bullied by the younger girls in the room. Biting her lip, she barely managed to suppress her laughter with a fake cough. On the other hand, she felt relieved. She had been secretly worried that Jimin might not adjust and would be left on the outskirts.

Minjeong regained her composure and, wearing latex gloves, began rummaging through Jimin's plastic storage bin. The box was filled to the brim with snacks like chocolate bars and sausages. It seemed she spent every cent of her prisoner funds on food, which made it all the more puzzling that her back, which Minjeong had touched earlier, was so gaunt and fragile. She had looked thin during the intake physical examination, but it seemed she had lost even more weight since then. Although Minjeong herself was quite slender, her lifestyle was entirely different from that of an inmate, so she couldn't help but feel concerned. On her days off, Minjeong went to Pilates and the gym for proper workouts, but the inmates, apart from a brief, scheduled walk, had to spend their days hunched over on the cramped floor mats of their cells. Perhaps that was why so many inmates actually gained weight before their release.

Setting her worries aside for a moment, Minjeong picked up a notebook from the bottom of the bin and shook it out, checking every corner of the pages. Not a single scrap of paper fell out. Just as she was about to give up, a sentence in the corner of an inadvertently opened page caught her eye.

[Officer Minjeong dropped the 'Officer' and called me Jimin-ssi.]

She froze for a moment. The bright, cheerful energy emanating from the firmly pressed handwriting made her stomach churn. Minjeong stared at the sentence intently before slowly turning her head to look at the hallway outside the open iron door. She could see Jimin’s back, her head pressed against the wall while her cellmates scolded her. This was Jimin’s diary. A sense of embarrassment and guilt, as if she had snooped into something she shouldn't have seen, washed over her, and she hurriedly closed the notebook and the lid of the storage bin. Standing up, Minjeong swung open the kitchen cupboard. Tucked between the stacks of plastic containers used by the inmates for their meals, a small red chili paste tub caught her eye. As she lifted the tub—which the inmates passed down as bowls—the three characters 'Yoo Jimin' written in crude black marker on the bottom came clearly into view. When she opened the lid, a spicy scent wafted out, and tucked inside was a rolled-up bundle of paper. Minjeong took it out and unfolded it. They were the prisoner fund receipts that Jimin had claimed were missing. It seemed she had hidden them safely away in her own precious, named food bowl. Minjeong let out a hollow sigh, glancing between the bundle of receipts in her palm and the chili paste tub. She looked toward Jimin, who was still being scolded in the hallway, and masked her complex, subtle emotions.

Suddenly, with a crackle of static, the speaker announced, "Inmates attending the religious gathering, please assemble in the hallway." Simultaneously, the mechanical sound of all the cell doors in the ward opening in unison filled the corridor. Minjeong stuffed the missing receipts back into the red tub and closed the cupboard. She then turned toward the open door of 3-Ha-15.

"3-Ha-15, everyone except Yoo Jimin-ssi, proceed to the religious gathering."

At Minjeong's order, the inmates of 3-Ha-15, who had been pressed against the wall, slowly turned and gave a polite bow to Minjeong, who was standing inside the room. Then, a cacophony of blessings was showered upon Jimin. "Oh Lord, Amen," "Namu Amita Bul, Gwanse-eum Bosal," "In the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, fighting, Jimin-unnie." Once the three inmates of 3-Ha-15 finished their prayers—entrusting Jimin to their respective deities—and rushed to the assembly line, the hallway instantly became a sea of people. As Jimin had been incarcerated for less than a month and was ineligible to apply for religious gatherings, she could not, by principle, be left alone in a multi-person cell. Normally, she would have to be temporarily moved to another room where the non-religious inmates were gathered. However, after confirming the backs of the inmates receding down the hallway, Minjeong called out to Jimin, who was still standing blankly in the doorway.

"Yoo Jimin-ssi, come inside."

"……."

"I said come inside."

Jimin walked in hesitantly and shut the iron door that had been propped open. *No, why would you do that?* Minjeong pressed a hand to her forehead. Every door in the prison automatically locked the moment it closed. It was designed so that it could never be opened from the inside; one had to press the call button and request the central control room to open it. Minjeong had intended to just give her a warning while leaving the door ajar, but the situation had spiraled. Swallowing a sigh, she thrust the stack of commissary receipts, which were kept inside a gochujang container, toward Jimin. The moment Jimin saw them, she hung her head low and apologized in a voice so faint it was barely audible.

"I’m sorry."

"I have absolutely no idea what you’re trying to do right now."

"……."

"Do you think I’m a pushover? Or is this place your personal playground, Yoo Jimin-ssi?"

Jimin’s head snapped up. She waved both hands frantically and shook her head, side to side. Her restless, blinking eyes and the corners of her mouth—drooping as if she were on the verge of tears—were etched into Minjeong’s sight.

"No, that’s not it at all. I’m sorry. I… I just… I’m sorry. I’m so sorry."

The Jimin portrayed in the media hadn’t been like this. When Yoo Jimin—the veiled illegitimate daughter—surpassed Yoo Hyemin, the officially recognized heir to the Sangmoon Group, and rose to the top of the succession line, the world had been turned upside down. The Jimin seen by the media had been an inscrutable, free-spirited person who stared into the cameras without a flicker of hesitation, even while being taken in for a warrant validity hearing. Watching Jimin’s thin shoulders tremble, Minjeong felt a complex, subtle emotion wash over her once again. From the drug charges to this very moment, she couldn’t understand any of it. Minjeong pulled off her stiff latex gloves, pressed her temples firmly, and slowly lowered her hands. Seeing Jimin standing right in front of her, trembling and repeating her apologies, Minjeong’s resolve buckled. An impulsive urge to comfort the crestfallen Jimin welled up inside her.

"Jimin-ssi."

"……Yes?"

Jimin looked up at Minjeong with wide, wet eyes. Minjeong glanced briefly out the living room window toward the corridor, then lowered her voice as she fixed her gaze back on Jimin.

"I want to make things comfortable for you whenever I can. Even if I said there wouldn't be any special treatment... you know how it is."

Even the prison warden made sure to visit Jimin at least once a day to check on her. No matter what kind of chaos she had caused on the outside, the fact remained that she was blood of the Sangmoon Group. While they shouted about fairness on the surface, the reality was that everyone, from the highest-ranking officials down, was walking on eggshells around Jimin and implicitly forcing special treatment upon her. Since Minjeong was also a cog in that system, it was her destiny to end up looking after Jimin differently, regardless of her assurances that there would be no favoritism. But what was shaking Minjeong’s heart now wasn't the warden’s orders or the group’s background. It was a complicated feeling—whether it was pity or something else entirely.

"If you keep doing things like this, there’s a limit to how much I can cover for you."

Minjeong muttered this as she nudged the gochujang container into Jimin’s arms. Jimin clutched the red container to her chest as if it were a treasure, her lips parting slightly. The way Jimin’s throat bobbed with a tremor was captured clearly in Minjeong’s vision.

"I won't ask why you did it. Please be careful from now on."

Unable to meet Jimin’s teary gaze directly, Minjeong looked away. The image of the bright, cheerful sentences she had seen in the diary earlier collided with the crestfallen Jimin standing before her, causing a stinging heat to spread in the corner of her chest. The tears clinging to the tips of Jimin's frantically blinking lashes looked ready to fall at any moment, and Minjeong barely managed to suppress the urge to reach out and pat Jimin’s shoulder. Placing the small gochujang container on the sink, Jimin spoke.

"I don't know why I'm like this either."

"Pardon?"

"Because I keep wanting to see you."

"The commissary receipts?"

"You, Officer."

A small cough escaped from her throat in an instant. It was an unexpected answer. Minjeong blinked her eyes frantically, completely unable to decipher the meaning of those words. Embarrassment welled up from her toes, causing her to pivot this way and that, unable to find a place for her gaze to land. She scratched the back of her head for no reason, fiddled with the crooked utility belt at her waist, and asked Jimin.

"Why me?"

The person who had been broken and crestfallen just moments ago had instantly put on a serious face. Jimin tilted her head slightly, as if pondering her answer, and spoke.

"Because you're pretty?"

At those three short words, heat rushed up over Minjeong’s face. The sound of her own heartbeat echoed to the very tips of her ears, leaving her with no mental capacity to retort. Even though she was just standing still, she stumbled as if her feet had tangled together. Jimin let out an "oops" and reflexively reached out a hand. Minjeong backed away, the distance between them hair-thin, and finally managed to open her mouth.

"……Get some rest."

Having spat out only that, she hurried toward the closed iron door and shoved the handle roughly, but there was no way the already locked door would budge. Feeling Jimin’s scorching gaze on her back, she busily fumbled for the call button beside the door and pressed it. A few seconds later, Supervisor Oh’s voice came through the speaker.

—Speak.

"Supervisor Oh, I... the door, please open the door."

—Officer Kim? Were you in there?

"Yes, it’s me. Please... hurry."

—Just a moment.

The moment she heard the *click* of the locking mechanism disengaging, Minjeong bolted through the door. She had completely forgotten the iron rule that an inmate must never be left alone in a solitary cell, and the fact that she was supposed to move Jimin to another room. A red heat rose up the back of Minjeong’s neck and around her ears as she ran down the hallway. Trying to calm her violently thumping heart, she repeated to herself over and over: That woman clearly hadn't fully slept off her medication.
